- Double laser from ledge: take the first laser, then dash shine before the next laser. Up tilt challenges ledge dash but not double laser.

- Do more running shine and run up shield. Too much drill — need to mix up and approach on the ground.

- RUNNING SHINE: gotta be in run, then quarter circle down, then B right after down. JC up smash is similar range.

- Keke keeps anti-airing with up tilt — need to stop full hopping so much because he's hard reading that. Up throw under the platform.

- Count how many times your opponent misses their tech in a specific situation. Then later you can read the missed tech.

- STOP NAIRING on the platform, bad option. Go for up air, or stay below the platform.

- Falco dash attacked your shield — it's NOT SAFE. Try to just up smash out of shield, or even shield grab. Recognize when Falco is doing bad pressure on your shield: up smash, nair or bair OOS, or Shine OOS.

- If the pressure is good, then roll OOS. Make sure to not exit shield if you don't need to. Pay attention to the pressure sequences. Need to be exiting shield DELIBERATELY.

- Dash dance closer to both spacies so you have more available openings besides full hop.

- Practice: running shine, up airs on the platform tech chase, OOS options.

- Punish — Order of operations: can we get a kill confirm? If not: high percent up throw up air / bair / up smash, 50/50 forward throw shine gimp, edge guard, combo. Up air, up smash, knock down tech chase. Up smash lands on the platform — be ready to waveland onto the platform.


- Position: how did I open up my opponent? Was it a clean hit? Did they get knocked down? Am I close enough to follow up?

- Focus on deliberate move choice. Stop nairing in places where it could have been a meaningful combo — go for up air or waveland onto platform. 

- Tech chase, read tech in place / missed tech, go for a mixup. (Cody Schwab just told Joe this in their lesson)


- Don’t double down if the opportunity has passed. Its a skill to recognize when its past. Don’t overextend. Be focused on the time window of when the opportunity is coming.

- You need to anticipate 15 frames in advance because that's about reaction time. Depending on the move, the character, and their threat range, sometimes this amount of time is even longer. 

- Grounded mixup (mid-short running shine-ish range ): do this a lot more. Find this range and stay there. Run up JC up smash, running shine, run up JC grab, run up shield.

- Once falco realizes fox is full hopping a lot, he wants to anti-air Fox more than Fox does in the ditto. Don’t full hop at ALL the time — he's looking to get you above him anyway. If you’re getting anti-aired too much, you're not establishing ground threat enough.

***- Do close range options more (short hop aerial also ok here if it breaks cc and is within a similar range): run up shield (safe), run up JC up smash, running shine, run up JC grab. Then once they’re threatened, you can full hop more because their mental stack has more mixups.***

- ALL of this depends on playing closer in that good mid-short range (running shine range).

- Screen looking: where should you be looking? How long do you have to react? Are you autopiloting? Is this situation actually reactable? Sometimes reading missed tech is better than reacting.

- Jumping off the platform is very reactable — don’t do that too much.

- Drill > grab is very strong. Drill grab is good if they attack out of lag vs shine or SDI up / spotdodge. Drill > up tilt is similar.

- Beat spotdodge by waiting a beat. Then dash dance up smash or full hop up air.

- DON’T FAIR so much. It’s like weak hit nair. Good for high percent combos, bad in neutral or low percent. F tilt is similar — also bad at low percent.

- Shine gimps are great. Use drill below stage if you can rinse repeat it in a way where they dont get the chance to tech on the wall — it's good.

- Triple jab was bad: first jab good, 2 and 3 bad. Instead of jab 2 → up smash / up tilt. Instead of jab 1 → hold space, CC reversal or dash back punish. 

- Shine was bad too because he snaps to ledge from that range.

- F smash is bad when they are on ledge. Run up shield instead — beats ledge hop dair, then punish OOS. If they double laser, try to jump shine.

- Focus on what opponent does from ledge: ledge dash, double laser, ledge hop dair.

- Stop chasing into the corner. Stay center while they’re cornered. Hold space and wait for them to push in. Sometimes holding space and letting them hang themselves while you are in a good position is better. 

- YOU HAVE A REACTION POINT AT THE APEX OF FULL HOP. This is why full hop is strong — it’s non-committal and a mixup with a reaction window.
Invincible opponent: GTFO. Play EXTREMELY REACTIVELY — you are DEFENDING. Full hop top platform, light shield on platform, ledge up B stall.

- Save states: Falco double laser from ledge, Falco dair from ledge.

***THE BAD NAIR — keep focusing on deleting this. ***

- What will my move lead to if it hits? If nothing, it’s bad. Example: sub 35% nair didn’t send into tumble → bad. Replace that nair with up air.

- Be SCARED to hit Falco on the platform. You’re not respecting this enough. Watch for shield drop shine — it’s frame 1.

- Watch where opponent breaks rules. If they get weak hit, you can CC punish.

- *** Improvement framework: Punish / Neutral / Defense — pick one focus from each for each analysis***

# Core Issues (Big Picture)

***The biggest problem right now is that your brain turns off in important moments. This shows up most clearly after dashback situations where you are not actually watching the screen, which means you miss reaction windows and fall back on habits like disengaging or throwing out nair. Because of this, you end up with too many situations where you don’t know what to do, and those moments turn into either panic hitboxes or giving up space unnecessarily.***

There is also a mismatch between how fast you are playing and how effective your openings are. You are playing at a very high speed, but not converting efficiently, while your opponent is getting far more value per opening. Your play naturally creates scrappy situations, but you are not capitalizing on them in a structured way, whereas a cleaner style focuses on fewer but more meaningful openings.

---

# Neutral Game and Positioning

***Your dash dancing is currently too far away, and you default to disengagement almost every time. The key change is to learn how to dash dance closer, even though it will initially get you hit more. That is part of the learning process. The goal is to position yourself so that you are just outside your opponent’s range, not fully retreating. Dashback should not be a way to escape — it should be a way to set up a counterhit by creating just enough space.***

While dash dancing, you need to actively watch your opponent. The purpose of the movement is to check their timing. Around 15–16 frames into your movement, you should be identifying what they are doing and preparing to re-engage. A major goal is to punish the end of their dash by aiming for the back of their movement, which can force them into a run state and remove their ability to dash back again.

You are also giving up space too easily and not taking space when it is offered. When your opponent yields ground, you need to move forward and claim it. Staying closer is important because it pressures your opponent into making mistakes. Right now, your spacing often puts you too far away to realistically threaten a punish, which removes that pressure entirely.

---

# Openings and Hit Selection

You are over-relying on nair and using too many hitboxes in general. This makes your gameplay predictable and reduces your ability to get meaningful openings. Instead, you should be looking for more direct options that allow you to stay close and immediately threaten a punish.

***Nair still has a role, especially in situations where you are unsure what to do, but it should lead into a tech chase rather than resetting the situation. One important adjustment is to stop using nair as a tech chase follow-up. The goal is to turn knockdowns into structured punish sequences, not more scrambling.***

---

# Punish Game

You are rushing your combos, even though you often have more time than you think. This causes you to drop conversions or choose suboptimal follow-ups. Slowing down is critical here. Focus on clean confirms and reaction-based extensions rather than trying to force speed.

Tech chasing needs to become automatic. After a nair knockdown or any grounded hit, your immediate focus should be on tracking and reacting. This includes practicing wavedash follow-ups and maintaining grounded control. These situations should feel like second nature rather than something you have to think through each time.

A major defensive gap right now is your lack of **ASDI down and crouch cancel (CC)** usage. You are not consistently holding down in situations where it would stabilize your position or allow you to counterattack. You should be holding down after missed moves and against weaker aerials, such as weak nair, so you can absorb the hit and immediately respond. Building strong CC habits will make scrappy exchanges far more favorable for you and prevent you from getting pushed out of situations where you could be winning.

---

# Decision Making and Awareness

A lot of your current issues come from autopilot decisions. You default to nair, dash away, or throwing out hitboxes without fully considering what your opponent is doing. Instead, you need to stay mentally engaged and constantly evaluate their intentions. Ask yourself what they are trying to bait and how you can punish it.

When you successfully bait an option, you need to already know the punish. For example, a standing grab has significant startup, and you should be prepared to react to it consistently. Recognizing these situations is not enough — you need to convert them.

---

# Movement and Mixups

You need to expand your movement options so that you are less predictable in close-range situations. Wavelanding through your opponent can create cross-ups, while wavelanding in place or slightly forward can counter undershoots. These options help you stay close without committing to unsafe approaches and give you more flexibility in how you apply pressure.

---

# Ledge Play

Your current ledge play is too hesitant, and you are giving away opportunities. When you grab the ledge and have a reliable ledgedash, you should prioritize getting off the ledge quickly rather than over-refreshing. It does not need to be perfect — even a lower GALINT ledgedash is sufficient in many situations.

In scrappy scenarios, speed matters more than perfection. Getting back on stage quickly gives you reversal potential and prevents your opponent from freely setting up. Against certain recovery patterns, you should be looking to ledgedash into aggressive options or even pass through your opponent to reposition.

---

# Mental Model Shift

Right now, your gameplay leans toward creating a lot of interactions, but not all of them are meaningful. A more refined approach focuses on fewer, cleaner openings with better positioning and awareness.

The key idea is to consistently put yourself in positions where your opponent is likely to make mistakes. By staying close and applying pressure, you encourage them to throw out hitboxes or commit to unsafe options. Your job is then to recognize those moments and punish them cleanly.

---

# Training Priorities

Your highest priority is to stay mentally engaged during movement, especially during dashback. You need to actively watch your opponent, react to their actions, and re-engage with intention. Alongside this, improving your use of ASDI down and crouch cancel will help stabilize your performance in scrappy situations and give you more consistent counterplay.

In neutral, focus on learning to dash dance closer, improving your whiff punish timing, and breaking the habit of automatic disengagement. Youre gonna get blown up for this, and that is okay. The goal isnt the result, the goal is to become the better player. In punish, slow down your decision-making and drill tech chasing until it becomes automatic. At the ledge, commit to immediate ledgedashes and accept that they do not need to be perfect to be effective.

You also need to actively remove habits that are holding you back, such as panic nairs, excessive hitbox usage, and giving up space without reason.

---

# Final Summary

You are already creating opportunities in your gameplay, but you are not consistently recognizing or converting them. The issue is not execution or speed, but clarity and awareness in key moments.

Improvement will come from slowing the game down mentally, even if your hands are still fast. By focusing on positioning, reacting more deliberately, and choosing cleaner options, you will turn your existing openings into reliable advantages. Watch this Cody Schwab vod vs Adwan and Moky to get a better sense of how to play the closer positions and the ways that Cody dash dances. If you want you could even compare it to the Snap vod you showed. Pay attention to his spacing and dash dance and how close he his to play this aggro style he plays.

notes:
Shep:
Yo, I watched your Aerius set. Actually really loving your play—just some stuff to go over at some point soon, hit me up.
I think you’re like a teeny mental adjustment away from rocking him lol.

Me:
Yeah, I’m down to talk soon for sure.

Shep:
Okay.
You’re looking good as hell by the way—crazy improvement.
It just looks like you don’t know what to do with it all.

Me:
I really don’t—that’s exactly right. I have many years of notes and I’ve been slowly building them up, but you can only lose to someone 20 times in a row before it really starts to bother you I guess lol.

Shep:
Yeah, it kind of gives off that “I can’t believe I’m playing this bad after all this” vibe, and I think that’s just wrong lol.

It feels like you’re practicing a slightly flawed system with a lot of good stuff in it.
You actually played well according to your plan—the plan itself is just a bit of an issue.

Me:
Yeah for sure, I know there are big gaps.

Shep:
It’s more mental than gameplay. Your situations were looking great.

It didn’t look like you were playing bad—it looked like you were executing well but missing the macro game that’s actually losing you the match.

Not even necessarily matchup-specific, more like a “how to play a set” type of plan.

Shep:
In the simplest terms, you’re just not manipulating your opponent.

You’re somewhat missing the two-player game elements—understanding the opponent’s mental weaknesses and how they feel.

It feels like you see them as:
“They’re just going to do good stuff,”
and then you’re the only one who has to really try or you’ll mess it up lol.

You’re doing things that are kind of bait-y sometimes, but you’re not actively baiting anything in particular or engaging with their emotions.

It’s like playing poker where you always bet based on your hand, instead of betting to manipulate the other player.

Me:
Right, totally. I kind of felt he was off-put after game 1 for sure, but I see what you’re saying. I’m super down to talk.
I see what you’re alluding to at least.

Shep:
Yeah, it’s a flow thing. It’s just an angle to the game that I can tell you aren’t including in your plan.

So you end up having to both execute the matchup AND compensate for being blind to that angle.

And people will subconsciously latch onto that weakness and start punishing it.

Short term, just imagine the opponent is way more nervous than you and trust your patterns. We’ll go into detail later.

Me:
I feel okay. I’m a little upset that I played pretty poorly against ICs because I’ve internalized your video concepts, but I missed my SDI on down throw down air, slight DI on up throw, and missed a meteor cancel. I felt like I was running low on gas by that point.

After I lost and we talked, I actually felt like I agree with you about not being disappointed. Today might be the first time where I can go home and feel like there’s some hope here.

The ICs stuff doesn’t worry me—I’m out of practice because I’ve been focusing on Arcadian matchups, and those are easy to fix with save states.

I was thinking about what you said about the two-player part of the game. I realized it was my turn to start threatening undershoot and overshoot aerials, but only after I finally put Aerius in a position where he felt pressure—and I realized it way too late.

Shep:
You actually hit the SDI on down throw down air multiple times. It’s not easy to get every time—I don’t think your DI was the problem.

The neutral exchanges are where you’re losing.

Shep:
It’s like if you were playing poker by always betting based on what your hand was, instead of betting to manipulate the other players.

You’re doing things that are bait-y, but you’re not actively baiting anything specific or engaging with their emotions.

Me:
I wish I understood how to properly pick what bait my opponent might take—and even when I do, I wish I could figure it out faster.

Thinking back, I feel like scary mixups could happen in spots like throws in the corner or off drill if I dash back and check, then actually act off that check instead of just guessing.

I’m not really sure what to do with the information that my opponent is staggered in Marth vs Fox. I don’t think I’ve consistently put him in a state where he feels real pressure until recently.

I’ve also conditioned myself to be ULTRA cautious, even in spots where I’m clearly in advantage.

“It’s Just Another You”

Shep:
You’ve got to think about what you would think if you were them.

You get baited, so they can get baited too.

It’s just another version of you on the other side with slightly different stats lol.

Me:
Okay, yeah—when you put it like that it makes way more sense.

It feels like if I apply this method, I could start outplaying people on a completely different axis of the game.

Shep:
Yep.
You’re actually shmooving lol.
You just don’t have anywhere practical to put it yet because you’re not using this layer.

Me:
“One step further” makes so much sense.

We don’t have to overcomplicate this—we just have to play exactly one step ahead so we can have a clear plan based on how they feel.

I think I try to play two steps ahead or zero steps ahead, and I probably get analysis paralysis from that.

Neurons are firing right now lol—this is starting to make a lot more sense.

I just need to practice this a ton in VOD review until it becomes subconscious.

Me:
My first instinct when someone is staggered is to smother them with something fast like frame 1 nair or running shine.

That works really well against worse players because I can brute force it.

But against better players, I feel like I overstep and get punished—like I’m breaking rules or forcing mixups from too far.

Are there certain emotional tells you look for more than others?
Or am I overthinking this and should just watch the set from their perspective?

Shep:
If you’re still getting the “they’re staggered” signal against Aerius, you can use it—but one step further.

When he’s staggered, he’s going to expect you to instant approach.

So what is he doing to counter that? Shield? Swing? Space?

Then:

What beats that?
When that gets beaten, how does he react?

You just respond to the signals based on what the player is capable of.

You already know he’s doing things to counter your instant attack—now you just have to identify what, and beat it.

Me:
I’ve been trying to raise the floor of my game outside of punish, and this makes so much sense.

I feel like I’ve been able to sense how my opponent is playing for a while—I just haven’t developed intuition for what to do with that information yet.

I still need to find the critical points where I can act on it.

Just need to think about what they want to do.

Shep:
Yeah, people tend to think about what the opponent wants in a purely theoretical way.

But it’s also just:
“When I’m Marth in that position, I feel really tempted to do this.”

The theory gives you a baseline, but you also need to understand the emotions and vibes behind their decisions.

-Wd oos need work: Specifically with upsmash oos -- you need to pay attention to how far you actually are when something feels whiff punishable

-Reacting to upthrow upsmash near the platform where if they DI toward the platform its a platform techchase, and if they DI away its an upthrow upsmash -- getting comfortable naturally with whats easier to execute when we feel like we are playing our C game

-Whiff punishing overshoot aerial with your own dashback --> something -- doesnt really matter (matters to some degree) be in range to whiff punish something -- "i/e I think my opponent is going to overshoot something here, therefore i will dash back). Dashback is good at beating aerial approach because drill is good at beating the grounded approaches. You dont wanna drill someone whos gonna land on the ground before you. 

-Grabbing ledge and identifying when its time to grab to ledge. We practice things like firestalling at appropreate timing, wavedash fastfall, multiple wavedashes in a row to grab the ledge

-Fast fall shine setups -- this can include the rinse repeat drill to make sure they cant wall tech -- being able nail things like armada shine or fastfall shine will make it so you have to play less recovery mixups

-Learn your throw mixups  -- if you can throw them off the stage and it sets up for something meaningful, throw them off the stage -- setting up a edgeguard is usually easier than setting up a tech -- sometimes the techchase or juggle kill confirm is better but ONLY if its a kill confirm. Get comfortable learning all the different DI varations on this mixup tree

-Learn your oos sheild options -- when is it time shine oos? When is it time to roll? When can we upsmash? -- Have good solution. 

-Get into pre tournament habits that let you recreate situations where you play well -- Good sleep, good warmup, good nutrition, == have a good pre tournament ritual that gets you to homestatisis
-Proper warmup -- Ask for a handwarmer! When handwarming, practice things that are kinda frame that you know you have, but you could drop if you havent done it on a day to day basis

-Start with solo practice -- practice the things you know you'll have to do in tourney later that day -- then play someone at your level or worse than you (nail punish before bracket), then play someone better than you (nail mental stack + autonomous punish)

playerName: Owl Reaction Point Notes (Ranked Set vs. AP)
notes:
- Owl says that I am struggling the most with making marth uncomfortable — can deal with specific marth walls with harder callouts if paying more attention to the reaction points and understand exactly how much time you have for those reaction points

- Most of the Problems come in the level two aka NOT whiff punishing but rather level one with the normal callouts

- Start with a soft callout like drill and then when you are confident you can more to a hard callout like dashdance or upsmash 

- ***Generically there are three types of marths: Dashback, downtilt and aerial marths *** -- in the example of this vod, AP employs a lot of downtilt, so lets talk about all three 

- Reaction point for downtilt should be 15 frames for reaction time to whiff punish and 9 frames for the actual whiff punish aka 25ish frames

- Why the downtilt archetype of marth is less common is because of the EV —  downtilting is usually not that great if Fox shows marth that is doing a good job of whiff punish the fair OR the downtilt as he pleases, which is a set of mixups within its own right, AND you have about 15 frames of leniency to punish the fair and it take a couple of frames to take space. For this reason, in the corner you want to do either undershoot or fadeback drill, because its a really good low risk callout. The fadeback drill is good against the mid and high level marths who will downtilt and then dash back or cross up, or maybe try to over shoot with something which are kind of coupled together because of the way he would be taking space. However, this fadeback drill might stop working against the tippy top level marths who are aware of the fact that marth doesnt have enough time to react to fox to fox doing fadeback drill and also do something about it because they would know not to try to interact with once fox shows them that they might do said fadeback drill because the mixup would no longer be in the favor of marth.

- Short hop drill is 22 frames so you cant do it on reaction to the end lag of something like downtilt and instead have to deal with it via reaction of how they choose to dash in at you (dash forward, wavedash, or sh).. Cant stay in the range of WD dtilt so get out preemptively or commit to a hard option with drill.

- The second type of marth has its own category of mixups based on a reaction point in the form of wavedash. Wavedash is another huge reaction point to have because we have to be ready to react 15 frames ahead of time and in order to accomplish our goal of being frame neutral. The biggest reaction points to note off the wavedash are wavedash downtilt and wavedash forward smash. WD Downtilt is either a hard callout or soft callout on about frame 7. Maybe they want to call out your dash dance with downtilt, in which case we wanna be ready and thinking about ASDIng down at 0 wayyy more just for a liiittle more frame advantage — especially because this makes spots where whether or not the punish is possible MUCH less ambiguous. As we know trying to fight marth in spots that are ambiguous is the LEAST ideal because we already have to present different mixup ranges to marth in neutral so being less confident in where they actually are gonna be is gonna make actually spacing and hitting the punish even harder than it needs to be. 

-We didnt really cover dashback marth reaction points in this lesson but I think conceptually the idea of marths dashback being really good at catching fox overshooting makes enough sense and implictly relates to why when we play neutral with marth we are constantly threatening each other with different mixup ranges because if we tried to hit them where they are or even are gonna be with a far enough overshoot they are always gonna whiff punish us if they are good. Maybe AP does this sometimes but this is less relevant in this note section as is moreso for like establishing that there is a third marth archetype for reaction points. The dashback marth also kinda bundles with the dash forward marth in the sense that the dash forward allows marth to maybe run cancel fsmash or dash attack on the basis that the dash forward is a mixup to dashing back from a certain distance. Idk this is kinda just part of marths kit thinking about it a little bit more but i guess some marths are more inclined to dash back way more for this reason. 

- On the above note, you might also need to account for dash attack which hits a little bit faster than the WD mixup but is within the scope of what a good marth player (i/e Aerius) might want to do in the mixup range within a similar group of options as the WD or dash forward mixups we've been talking about.

- Owl says that for the scraps you need to not be close them or be ready for the forward smash as a mixup.

- Need to be ready to react to when marth wavelands on the platform since its the only stimulus you get between you and him maybe downtilting you 35 ish frames later. The reason we need to shift reaction points early to platform waveland is because 99% marth players are gonna forward air after the waveland from the platform so we can hard call them out.

- 15 to 20 is frames is about the time you to be able to distinguish the difference between short hop and full hop for marth

- Uptilt is actually a really bad anti air against marth. The only time it is ever good is if they land directly on top of you and even then they could land on top of you with a preemptive aerial as a mixup. Do bair instead if you want to anti air them or even shark them if you are in position!

- Move choice instead of upair in neutral also needs work

-Mentally be prepared to win neutral and ready to cover the DI — I am acting too soon without taking information on the screen.

-E/g After hitting aerius with the first drill, check if the drill is gonna connect to do something and think about what he might want to do after the drill

-Instead of a second drill, we have to think about where they are gonna be. Get out of the “monkey see monkey do”. 

-“What will he do to beat drill → dashback grab”. If i try to grab him out of the dash he could grab me cause his grab is faster.

-Try to whiff punish *something* by doing something that makes him think he has time to take to see if he actually has advantage. 

-On upthrow hard combo di at low percent set to always SDI → dash, fullhop, let go of stick to neutral pull back upair. We want to make sure he cannot SDI to the right so he cant get out. Two types of players in terms of SDI, Ones who try to SDI the way you go if you have full momentum. I/e someone who doesnt have good control of their fullhop upair drift so they will go away so that they hit the first hit and the second combo. 

-Because fox is dashing towards the marth and has a lot of momentum in one way what the marth will do in hope of keeping your momentum is they will SDI against your momentum to get out of the second hit of the upair

-My objective is to take space — if i can corner my opponent, I can win the game. If the person is dashing up to hit me to take space, we need to dash forward dash back. After that point, if you thought that they were gonna hit you for taking the space what should happen is you should be able to hit them for doing something preemptively but because you’ve dash backed and they've tried to hit you for preemptively and in lag

-If you think nair can get dashbacked even farther, you do the overshoot downtilt, otherwise you can try to dash forward and then dash back. Important to stay in tight dash dance. If we think that we suck at giving up a ton of space, just dash forward and dont even attack — if you think they arent gonna hit you immediately for taking the space, take the space. When you see a person whiff punishing you for an aerial that means that if you try to dash up to him and try to whiff punish you can take that space, dash in instant and hit them because you truly believe that they are letting you take space. 

-You do a dash up and marth likes to hit out of you taking space — what you can do: dash forward dash back, OR if you think they are gonna hit you at the delayed timing. If they hit you at the latter portion of the nair when you take space it means they are hitting you late, which means you can hit them out of it. 

-If i think marth is gonna dashback pivot grab we want to backflip instead of running shine . This is kinda the spot where they “cut you off” for dashing forward. 

-Take space more often (dash forward but do not dash back) when aerius is the one in my face — if i take the space and dont attack its similar to the dashback dash forward concept. This usually happens when he thinks im gonna be patient and do nothing. 


Outtakes:

DONT do long dash dances when you think they are gonna fsmash, we have to do a TIGHT dash dance to punish them without getting hit. The reason you do a long dash dance is to bait someone but if they are planning on playing at an unreactable range anyways its doesnt matter if you are baiting them, 
Pay attention to spacing and dash dance to get in closer — figure out how to deal with him dashing towards center

Figure out how to make your reaction point — this is where you are checking to see what happen.

Dash forward dash back — I saw hes trying to play patient, so im reading him dashing back by dashing forward, therefore cornering my opponent. 

Know that everything stems from our opponent trying to hit us for taking space, therefore when they try to hit us for taking the space, we can pretend to take the space (i/e dash forward dash back) and THEN whiff punish them for it.

Aerius not too campy, so no need to take space too often. More down to overshoot downtilt in that ambiguous spacing or overshoot nair, or dash forward dash back

Ledgedash — stand still for a second, could spotdodge. 

Jump back more often in the spot — can always refresh with jump back if you think they will do instant up b — next level is playing around the jump back stall by drifting back and hitting him for the jump back stall. For now, jump back stall — now you are invincible. 

Ledge Options:
Against ledgedash uptilt — turnaround full hop back air OR wait on the platform (sheild drop tomahawk grab)

Against side b to ledge: Stand, backflip shine spike the side OR if you think they are gonna double jump aerial, full hop bair whiff punish, could also try fastfall to the ledge double jump bair reading the side

- Against DK in shield in pressure at 0 — shine into full hop away if we are calling out mashing shield grab

Upair is a low to mid percent combo route because it combos into itself and uptilt and you can even shark dk landing and kill him. 
- We stop Upairing like 70 or 80 because literally every other move we have will win in an air to air or a ground to air that is not an up b out of shield, therefore things like nair and bair become a lot stronger
- MUST RESPECT GETUP ATTACK FROM THE LEDGE!!! CAN KILL YOU!
- DI Cargo throw on top plat out + away + SDI UP / Maybe also DI behind sometimes 
- Starting 40ish — running shine into nair seems really obnoxious (consider upair as well if

- Can jump out of cargo throw regrab around 49% on NO DI
- Falling upair pressure on the platform as a mixup to fullhop bair pressure
- Theres a low bair that can cover all of DK’s recovery mixups
- Shine upsmash good because dk is a big body
- Laser especially good if we dont want to contest shield drop bair. Reason we dont want to contest frame 1 shield drop bair

DI to top plat — eat first hit, then DI to side
- Running from too far at DK will result in getting baired in place — dont make it too obvious when we full dash across the screen
Slideof di — most important way to get out of DK combos
- Downsmash works well on rising up b recovery on yoshis (Maybe BF?), Spaced around the start of the rising up b (Similar to the roll up from the ledge timing)
- Waveshine downtilt when you think DK is gonna DI in to grab the ledge! 
- Run downtilt seems strong in neutral over running shine once it knocks down (24%) 
- Dk has a hard time covering ledge UNLESS he’s already on the ledge AND fox is below the stage
- If both of these are true, he has a pretty rinse repeat edgeguard on fox but otherwise covering the UP B to ledge mixup is obnoxious for DK 
- If we see DK is double jumping a lot we can try to play the air to air more
- Sometimes DK has to double jump in neutral to threaten a farther distance falling bair (Or stomp)
- Shine → bair → downsmash seems like a pretty good edgeguarding flowchart
- Punish tournament winner double jump (they usually do this when they fuck up whatever other ledge option they were trying to do, + DK’s tournament winner appears to be very reactable, kinda like ganon).
- Dash attack upsmash works very late

-OVERSHOOT

-Stop shine backairing

-If we have a running shine combo that we think the marth player is going to slide off at the ledge, we need to either:

-A: Shine wavedash jab shine to carry marth to the corner (Jab is great as an anti air specific 

OR

-B: Dash Jc shine / double shine to fuck up slide off timing 

-Try run up shield 

-DONT shine as an anti air

-Double Jump backward up-b instead of trying to super sweet ledge with side b every time to keep marth more honest

-Try adding SOME amount of hard overshoot in addition to in place / slight undershoot (Read dashback after aerial?, E/G:reading fair dashback

-Have to show the marth you could be down to overshoot all the way into the corner otherwise they will be down to always dashback into the corner.

playerName: How to beat floaty mid tiers conceptually -- Billy Notes
notes:
- Mid tiers - they have less relevant options. Sit down and map out their real options. 

- I can sit here and teach someone how to approach a mid tier matchup in an hour — look at every move they have, what the character’s kit make their goals?

- Stop overgeneralizing — fight DK by knowing his linear neutral you can just wait and laser and then do things that make it hard to interact — DK close range doesn't get much off of anything when facing forward

- X character has Y weakness and therefore because of X character and Y weakness I must play Z way.

- Make generalizations that allow you perceive the information correctly — the generalizations are not the answers themselves but instead the path to the answers. 

- i/e luigi’s main weakness is that he doesnt have horizontal drift means that the angles he can approach from is limited. As fox with a unlimited movement, you can outmaneuver the luigi player. In this case the thought process is outmaneuvering luigi by making it hard to find approach options. 

- Make savestates of the whole cast approaching 

- Is there a way that I can decide to engage with this character? If so, how? If not, how do I minimize the engagement rate?

- Mid tiers are mid tiers because they are more vulnerable, and you can force situations where engagement is impossible. You can simply run away, and sometimes they are unable to stop you because of their kit. Picking proper engagement points is a key driving principle in theory for beating mid tier characters.

- Stay in slight advantage until you find a spot to engage in. 

- Respect the Floaty mid tier kit — but dont respect the non functional moves. Know what is and isnt good in their kit and exploit them for doing the bad things. Understand the character dynamic. Figure out the character weakness and engage with them properly(which implies correct timing). 

- Know what is and isnt shield grabbable. Understand inherent qualities of moves and their various applications. (Fox nair, falco shine, marth nair, sheik fair when l canceled). Setup options in training mode and check if its safe. Understand what characters can do after a hit on shield. Sometimes you can just press the A button instead of shine oos. 

- Figure out where the mid tier has no options and follow up and abuse punishes as a result of knowing that character cant do anything when you counterhit. They have to work really hard to do anything when you engage with their weaknesses directly. 

- Whiff landing lag of back air against donkey kong — this character doesnt have x option which makes y option more reasonable. Another example could be samus downsmash having way more lag than initially appears.

- Do a variety of things — and don’t do the same thing.  Neutral is actually what is difficult for these characters, therefore once you do the same thing and they catch on they can do the thing where they potentially hit you really hard which is what makes them a mid tier and not a low tier.

- Full hop empty land and check what marth does — this faster than aerial and gives you visual time to confirm

- You cant ever hit anything marth does will full hop unless you are preemptively doing. This is part of the challenge against marth in neutral. 

- Focus on knowing when marth has to land — the spots that are coming up are spots where marth has to land on the ground — these are the moments where i need to whiff punish marth or pressure him. If you dont have the frames to directly punish him you need to setup for pressure.

- Apply aggressive dashback to whiff punish or pressure in these situations. 

- Recognize when you have a meaningful hit, but a direct combo, and what mixup needs to be played accordingly. Super important concept for fighting marth. 

- Be more willing to throw out hitboxes in the corner ONCE YOU HAVE MARTH IN THE ZONE NEAR THE INNER LIP. The difference between marth and falco in the corner is that you cant get away with walling pressure — you have to pick a move some times and dash out. 

- Full hop drift neutral or drift away at marth — never drift at marth, if you he will ALWAYS get away first and the moves hes has are too fast + dashback

- As soon as you seen marth do a normal the opportunity to whiff punish that is already over. Internalize that you need to read marth doing something to hit him or change gears to pressure or position.

- If you are not sure if your shine will connect cleanly or at all, waveshine down.

-  If you are close enough to marth and he doesnt know whats happening, if marth is dashing toward you while you are dashing back it is not great because the decision tree becomes much more complicate. 

- What makes dash back offensively good, when marth is in foxes threat he is constantly in a place where you cant react to foxes aerials. It sets up the time to if marth tries to stop you offensively you will be ready to hit him for it.,

- If you are going to laser, stop picking offensive option after its detrimental.

The process:

1. Start off by doing the thing in the easiest way i/e waveshine upsmash on shiek

The List:

- The most important thing for decisionmaking and execution in any given situation in melee. Fully understand it, and it will be easier to look at. Learn each situation over and over again. 

- Play the RPS and then sprinkle everything else in.

- There are other reasons why you should be beating people besides just adapting. 

- Practice aerial dashback because otherwise aerial shine / aerial isnt on your opponents mental stack 

- Stock lead means nothing in the ditto — percents matter a lot — stock leads only matter if the game goes to time and game never goes to time in fox dittos.

- Percent matters if your opponent is higher than yours because if you just trade hits, you just win the game. Not necessarily what you want to be looking for, you want clean neutral because that situation is independent of the stock lead. 

- Make your opponent prove that you have to be the one to approach, but that is never the case because of laser (i/e peach puff). Think more about what are good positions to be in rather than I have a lead and that my opponent needs to approach or that I’m behind so I need to approach.

- Make your opponent force you to have a reason to approach rather than be pressured by attrition.

- Approach when you need to approach and dont approach when you dont need to

- Take account of position and percent. 

- De group your fullhops (they are not grounded or actionable)

- If your opponent is just existing and you are full hopping you are at a mild disadvantage.

- Make sure each fullhop is individually fine and that you are not in fullhop mode. Have a good reason for each fullhop. 

- No reason to start drill high (unless you do fh drill and it combos on the ground) because you are aiming for your moves to hit and not whiff. By playing you either a: decrease disadvantage or b: find an advantage state

- Attempt to always make decisions as humanly late as possible. Ideally every input you do should have been decided 15 frames before you do it. Kinda hard to do perfectly but what you should be aiming to do on a macro level.

- Sometimes its okay to hold a good position and you dont have to attack. As soon as you do something punishable you are no longer in an advantage state and can die. Therefore if you wait while you are in advantage and force your opponent to initiate and lose. As long as I play patient in advantage the risk reward is skewed in my favor.

- Replace giving up a lot of space to bait the opponent to move forward with aerial dashback or fullhop in place. 
If you are trying to corner pressure an opponent, give up space but not enough space to stop threatening your opponent.

- Don’t bait your opponent — it is a more inconsistent way to play the game rather than just playing the position in a more simple manner. Wait and screenlook because the opponent needs to fight out of the corner regardless. When you have frame advantage, you know your opponent has to do something because they are in more lag than you are. 

- Practice staying in whiff punishey range. Stop making hard reads they are just straight up bad risk reward. It is basically just a very hopeful guess. You have to have an exact read for that to be effective so instead play at whiff punish range. 

- Look for clean neutral wins where if the opponent does something you can punish and if you dont you are still generally in advantage 

- Each time you look for a hit ask yourself “did I have the prerequisite advantage to do the thing I did?” and also “did I deserve to get a hit here?” if you ask yourself this and you say no, you probably shouldn't be attacking. 

- Be more careful about your move choices “does this move lead to a punish?” “does this move get me punished?”

- Stop giving opponents who are just waiting for free hits 

- You are putting yourself in good positions but then you attack out of them and lose out on the value. Work on waiting to captialize in these spots.

- Start getting hit because you are not doing anything. When this happens, the opponent won the neutral game without getting a free hit, which is super duper sick.

-Be ready to disengaged if you’re not ready for the next interaction i/e dj to plat. This kinda goes hand in hand with stopping forcing spots and playing good positions

- Especially off of the winning neutral with a forward throw you should face away at the ledge — encourages side b, which makes uptilt edgeguards much more reliable. If you think they are gonna side b, now you can cover it better.

- Dont miss the punish on the tournament winner or you will get reversalled for trying to punish tournament. Punish tournament winner by reading either di in or di out. Try to hit them — (shine because it always knock down). Have a game plan to punish tournament winner is the main point. 

- Scout out dash back dash in jump out armada to cover jump back up b. Also, Rainy also doesnt cover jump back up b 

- Map out the forward throw mixup — what beats every mixup? Lots of foxes in norcal beat me really bad for this.


-  Ledgedash uptilt is good if they are gonna do full hop aerial or unspaced aerials. Often time you just put yourself in lag and then sometimes you just play the normal interaction

-  Charge a tiny bit on the 90ish hard di out kill confirm with upthrow runcancel upsmash
